article.title: IISc Physicists Observe a Dirac Fluid in Ultraclean Graphene, Breaking the Wiedemann-Franz Law by More Than 200-Fold
article.summary: Researchers at the Indian Institute of Science report electrons in ultraclean graphene flowing as a nearly perfect quantum fluid, with heat and charge transport decoupling by over two orders of magnitude at low temperatures.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

A team at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) in Bengaluru, working with collaborators at Japan's National Institute for Materials Science, has reported direct observation of a so-called Dirac fluid in ultraclean graphene, a state in which electrons abandon their individual particle behavior and flow collectively like a nearly frictionless liquid. According to a [ScienceDaily release](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm) published on April 15, 2026, the measurements show a breakdown of the Wiedemann-Franz law, a textbook principle that links electrical and thermal conduction in metals, by a factor of more than 200 at low temperatures.

The underlying study, "Universality in quantum critical flow of charge and heat in ultraclean graphene," appeared in Nature Physics and is led by Aniket Majumdar and professor Arindam Ghosh of the IISc Department of Physics, as described in an [EurekAlert release from IISc](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1096574).

## What We Know

The Wiedemann-Franz law, formulated in the 19th century, states that in ordinary metals the ratio of thermal to electrical conductivity is proportional to temperature via a universal constant. The IISc group reports that this proportionality collapses in graphene tuned to its Dirac point, the energy where the material sits at the boundary between metal and insulator. According to [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm), deviations from the law exceeded a factor of 200 at low temperatures, with electrical and thermal conductivity moving in opposite directions rather than tracking one another.

The researchers attribute this decoupling to a hydrodynamic regime in which electrons interact strongly with one another and behave more like molecules in a liquid than like independent carriers. As reported by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html), the measured viscosity of this electron fluid is extraordinarily low, described as roughly one hundred times less viscous than water and among the closest laboratory realizations of a so-called perfect fluid. The same source notes that the group analyzed the regime in two companion papers in Nature Physics, with DOIs 10.1038/s41567-025-02972-z and 10.1038/s41567-025-02974-x.

The Dirac fluid is often compared to the quark-gluon plasma generated in heavy-ion collisions at facilities such as CERN, another system whose collective flow approaches the limit of a perfect fluid. In the IISc experiments, the hydrodynamic behavior is governed by universal quantum parameters tied to the conductance quantum, a fundamental constant of nature, according to the [EurekAlert release](https://www.eurekalert.org/news-releases/1096574). Professor Ghosh is quoted by EurekAlert observing that "there is so much to do on just a single layer of graphene even after 20 years of discovery."

The effect is accessible only in extremely clean samples and near the charge neutrality point, conditions that the IISc group achieved using high-purity graphene sandwiched between hexagonal boron nitride layers supplied by collaborators Kenji Watanabe and Takashi Taniguchi at Japan's National Institute for Materials Science, as detailed by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm).

## What We Don't Know

The reported measurements do not by themselves settle the theoretical picture of how strongly correlated electrons in graphene transport heat. The group frames the result as evidence of a quantum critical, universal regime, but whether the same scaling persists across other two-dimensional Dirac materials, such as topological surface states or twisted bilayer systems, remains an open question. The sources cited do not indicate device-engineering applications for the decoupling effect; the immediate significance is for fundamental condensed-matter physics and for tests of hydrodynamic models borrowed from high-energy physics.

It is also unclear from the coverage whether independent groups have reproduced the quantitative 200-fold Wiedemann-Franz deviation. Earlier experiments had reported smaller violations in graphene near the Dirac point, and the new claim rests on the cleanliness of the samples and the precision of the simultaneous thermal and electrical transport measurements reported in Nature Physics.

## Why It Matters

Hydrodynamic electron flow has moved from theoretical curiosity to an active subfield of condensed-matter physics over the past decade, as cleaner two-dimensional materials have made it possible to see electrons behave collectively rather than as isolated quasiparticles. The IISc result, as summarized by [Phys.org](https://phys.org/news/2025-09-graphene-reveals-electrons-frictionless-fluid.html), pushes that program further by tying the transport behavior of a room-temperature material to universal quantum constants, and by connecting laboratory graphene experiments to the same mathematical framework physicists use to describe the quark-gluon plasma produced in particle colliders.
